edge: Graph-based methods are extensively applied to whole slide histopathology image analysis to model the spatial relationships among different entities.
Existing methods focus on modeling WSIs with homogeneous graphs but are unable to capture complex structural relations between biological entities.
A novel heterogeneous graph-based framework is proposed to leverage inter-relationships among different types of nuclei for WSI analysis.
WSI is formulated as a heterogeneous graph with a 'nucleus-type' attribute to each node and a semantic similarity attribute to each edge.
A new heterogeneous-graph edge attribute transformer (HEAT) is presented to leverage edge and node heterogeneity during message aggregating.
A new pseudo-label-based semantic-consistent pooling mechanism is designed to obtain graph-level features and mitigate the over-parameterization issue of conventional cluster-based pooling.
A causal-driven approach is proposed to improve the interpretability by attributing the contribution of each node, overcoming limitations of existing association-based localization methods.
Extensive experiments on three public TCGA benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
The framework is tested through extensive experiments on TCGA benchmark datasets.
The framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods on various tasks.

Histopathology slides provide rich information for treatment planning of cancer diseases.
Technological advancements in tissue digital scanners facilitate the development of whole slide histopathology image analysis.
Deep learning techniques play an important role in accurate and automated analysis of WSIs.
Since it is difficult to fit the complete WSI into memory, most works adopt multiple instance learning.

WSIs are represented as graphs of instances using GNNs
CNNs focus on locality in Euclidean space, while GNNs focus on locality on graph topology for analyzing deep connections in image data

Relational information learned by GNNs is important for prognosis in WSI analysis
Existing paradigms focus on representing WSI with a homogeneous graph structure for analysis
WSI analysis is done using GNNs with cluster-based pooling for predicting responses
Despite successes, methods using GNNs with cluster-based pooling suffer from several drawbacks
GNNs on homogeneous graphs focus on aggregating direct relational information from neighboring nodes, where the complex relational information of the graphs is often neglected.
For different graphs, the clusters defined by similarities between node embeddings have inconsistent meanings.
GNNs tend to easily overfit due to a lack of identifiability.
A novel framework for WSI analysis leverages a heterogeneous graph to learn the inter-relationships among different types of nodes and edges.
The heterogeneous graph introduces a 'nucleus-type' attribute to each node, which can serve as an effective data structure for modeling the structural interactions among the nuclei in the WSI.
To tackle the aggregation process in the heterogeneous graph, a novel heterogeneous-graph edge attribute transformer (HEAT) architecture is proposed.
The diverse structural relations among different biological entities in the WSI can be incorporated to guide the GNN for more accurate prediction.
To obtain the graph-level representations for slide-level prediction, a semantic-consistent pooling mechanism is proposed.

PL pooling pools node features to the graph level based on clusters with a fixed definition. This mechanism can regularize the graph pooling process and distill context knowledge from a pretrained model to alleviate over-parameterization.

Granger causality based localization method is proposed to identify potential regions of interest with clinical relevance, providing insights to pathologists and enhancing the clinical usability of the approach.
The method is evaluated on three TCGA public benchmark datasets including COAD, BRCA, and Camelyon 16 datasets from the TCGA project, comparing to various latest state-of-the-art methods.
WSI analysis approaches generally adopt MIL, which divides the WSI into fixed-size patches and compresses the information of these patches into low-dimensional vectors.
Conventional methods aggregate bags of instances to learn WSI-level features for final predictions.
Tellez et al. compress the WSI-level image into embedding vectors and use a standard CNN to perform patch-level and WSI-level cancer classification.
Tellez et al. compress the WSI-level image into embedding vectors and use a standard CNN to perform patch-level and WSI-level cancer classification.
Graph-based methods model the interactions between instances via graphs, providing more flexibility to analyze complex structures of WSIs.
Chen et al. propose patch-GCN, a method of modeling WSI with homogeneous graphs.
Chen et al. regress survival data with a graph convolutional neural network (GCN).
Zheng et al. propose a graph-based MIL method using graph transformer networks.
A recent method is proposed to model WSIs with heterogeneous graphs, introducing heterogeneity in each patch by different resolution levels.
The recent method only considers the resolution level heterogeneity of patches, with insufficient ability to model the complex contextual interaction between patches in the same resolution level.

We introduce a novel pooling method - pseudo-label (PL) pooling, to aggregate information with respect to the pseudo-labels (i.e., node types) predicted from a pretrained teacher network.

We introduce a novel pooling method - pseudo-label (PL) pooling, to aggregate information with respect to the pseudo-labels (i.e., node types) predicted from a pretrained teacher network (e.g., HoverNet [12]).

Unlike conventional methods of pooling features based on clusters, we define clusters using a pretrained node classifier. Pooling from pseudo-labels ensures the semantic consistency in cluster definitions and distills the context knowledge (e.g., nuclei features) from the teacher network.

Specifically, for each node type a, we pool all node features belonging to type a into a single vector ha with a readout layer. The pooled features from each node type are then aggregated into a feature matrix S ∈ R

We make use of the Granger causality to outline causal regions in the WSI with the causal graph explainer.
Given a trained GNN model M, the causal contribution of each node v is calculated using a specific formula.
The relationship between the node v and L(y, ˜yG) is defined as the calculation of difference between the true label y and the predicted label based on input graphs G.
The node v can be visualized with the causal contribution computed for each patch in the causality heatmap.
Addressing causality in instance interpretation can adjust for observational and selection biases, leading to improved explanation accuracy.
The causal property of the explainer could facilitate pathologists in finding potential biomarkers for diagnosis and prognosis by highlighting patches with clinical relevance in the WSI.
